question_title: Will ULA have at least one Vulcan launch attempt before May 1, 2026?
summary: ULA's Vulcan rocket is grounded after its 2nd identical SRB nozzle burn-through (Feb 12, 2026 USSF-87 mission). The Space Force paused all NSSL launches, with the investigation expected to take "many months." The precedent from the Oct 2024 anomaly shows a 5-month certification review minimum. The question window (Feb 17 - Apr 30) is only ~2.5 months — far too short for a typical anomaly investigation resolution, especially for a recurring systemic problem. No zero-SRB missions are manifested, and there are no indications of imminent clearance. The probability is low but not negligible, accounting for small tail scenarios like unexpectedly fast investigation or a theoretical zero-SRB mission.

factors.description: Space Force paused all NSSL launches on Vulcan; Col. Zarybinsky says investigation could take 'many months'
All future Vulcan launches on hold pending investigation (practical reality: all manifested missions need SRBs)
Precedent: After Oct 2024 SRB anomaly, certification review took 5 months; first return flight was 10 months later
Recurring systemic problem: 2nd identical SRB nozzle burn-through in 4 flights (50% anomaly rate), requiring thorough investigation
Analyst estimates (NextBigFuture) of 3-6+ months delay, extending well past the May 1 deadline
No near-term Vulcan missions manifested for zero-SRB configuration; all payloads (military and Kuiper) require SRBs
Strong institutional pressure to resume flights (military GPS/OPIR needs, Amazon Kuiper FCC deadline July 2026) could theoretically accelerate
Vulcan has certified zero-SRB configuration (VC0S), creating theoretical but unmanifested path to launch without resolving SRB issue

reasoning:
**Forecast: 10% probability (logit: -2.2)**

The evidence strongly favors NO. Vulcan is grounded after a recurring SRB anomaly, with official estimates of "many months" for the investigation — far exceeding the ~2.5-month resolution window. The 10% accounts for small-probability scenarios like an unexpectedly fast investigation resolution or a zero-SRB mission materializing.

resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if ULA posts an official update before May 1, 2026 indicating that a Vulcan mission had a launch attempt on or after February 17, 2026 and before May 1, 2026 (including a late scrub where the attempt is documented by ULA as a launch attempt). This question resolves to no otherwise. The primary source is ULA’s Vulcan page at https://www.ulalaunch.com/rockets/vulcan-centaur
fine_print: A “launch attempt” must be documented by ULA as an attempt (for example, an official mission update describing a scrub from a count). Routine schedule posts without an attempt do not count. If ULA’s Vulcan page is not updated, resolve using ULA’s official news/press releases for Vulcan missions, and if needed resolve disputes using credible sources per https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitionswhile prioritizing ULA statements. Candidate Sources: ULA news https://www.ulalaunch.com/news
background_info:
Vulcan flight cadence is a key constraint for U.S. launch capacity and national security launches.
